pick.rationale: BYU enters with a significant analytical advantage, ranking 23rd in KenPom and 33rd in Torvik with an elite offense (10th in adjusted offensive efficiency) that should exploit NC State's porous defense (226th in defensive rating). The Cougars' 88th-ranked defense is substantially better than NC State's, and BYU tested itself nightly in the Big 12 with an 8-8 record against tournament teams. NC State survived a First Four game against Texas but now faces a quick turnaround against a superior opponent, and their defensive vulnerabilities (allowing 76.5 PPG) will be exposed by BYU's balanced scoring attack led by freshman AJ Dybantsa and sophomore Rob Wright III. The analytical gap of roughly 30 spots between these teams, combined with BYU's rest advantage and NC State's defensive deficiencies, gives the 6-seed a clear path to victory.

Thursday’s Big 12 Tournament quarterfinal loss to No. 5 Houston offered another example of how those elements factor into BYU’s performance as the team prepares for the NCAA Tournament. “I think we’ve found our identity as a team,” sophomore guard Rob Wright III said. “We’ve got role players stepping up, like (Dominique Diomande) and Aleksej (Kostić) hitting big shots for us. Just figuring out who we are as a team.” Throughout the season, BYU has leaned heavily on freshman AJ Dybantsa and Wright, especially following the late‑season injury to senior guard Richie Saunders, who produced roughly 20% of the teams offense. Photo by Rodrigo Zarate Both remained central against Houston. Dybantsa extended his streak of 20‑point games with 26 points, while Wright provided early momentum with first‑half scoring and ball handling under pressure. BYU’s most efficient stretches, however, came when additional contributors were consistently involved. “I’m a big believer in God. I knew my moment would come this year, next year, two years,” Diomande said. “You’d see me in the gym even when I wasn’t playing. This opportunity, I was ready for it. Just grateful to be in this position.” Kennard Davis Jr., Kostić and Diomande all played notable roles in the first half. Davis added timely scoring, Kostić hit back‑to‑back 3‑pointers that helped BYU take a momentum building lead, and Diomande delivered key defensive stops. Photo by Rodrigo Zarate Defensively, BYU continued the improvement it has shown in recent games, including its second‑round win over West Virginia. The Cougars pressured the ball effectively, contested shots at the rim and forced turnovers that created several first‑half momentum swings. Keba Keita’s interior presence and improved perimeter rotations helped BYU manage Houston’s physical style and limit extended scoring runs. BYU played with a scrappy approach that has become a growing part of its identity in recent weeks, relying on a back‑to‑basics defensive scheme that allowed them to stay competitive against Houston. “I thought our defense for the most part was good,” coach Kevin Young said. The defensive effort allowed BYU to take a halftime lead, but the second half became a possession‑by‑possession battle. Thursday’s Big 12 Tournament quarterfinal loss to No. 5 Houston offered another example of how those elements factor into BYU’s performance as the team prepares for the NCAA Tournament. “I think we’ve found our identity as a team,” sophomore guard Rob Wright III said. “We’ve got role players stepping up, like (Dominique Diomande) and Aleksej (Kostić) hitting big shots for us. Just figuring out who we are as a team.” Throughout the season, BYU has leaned heavily on freshman AJ Dybantsa and Wright, especially following the late‑season injury to senior guard Richie Saunders, who produced roughly 20% of the teams offense. Photo by Rodrigo Zarate Both remained central against Houston. Dybantsa extended his streak of 20‑point games with 26 points, while Wright provided early momentum with first‑half scoring and ball handling under pressure. BYU’s most efficient stretches, however, came when additional contributors were consistently involved. “I’m a big believer in God. I knew my moment would come this year, next year, two years,” Diomande said. “You’d see me in the gym even when I wasn’t playing. This opportunity, I was ready for it. Just grateful to be in this position.” Kennard Davis Jr., Kostić and Diomande all played notable roles in the first half. Davis added timely scoring, Kostić hit back‑to‑back 3‑pointers that helped BYU take a momentum building lead, and Diomande delivered key defensive stops. Photo by Rodrigo Zarate Defensively, BYU continued the improvement it has shown in recent games, including its second‑round win over West Virginia. The Cougars pressured the ball effectively, contested shots at the rim and forced turnovers that created several first‑half momentum swings. Keba Keita’s interior presence and improved perimeter rotations helped BYU manage Houston’s physical style and limit extended scoring runs. BYU played with a scrappy approach that has become a growing part of its identity in recent weeks, relying on a back‑to‑basics defensive scheme that allowed them to stay competitive against Houston. “I thought our defense for the most part was good,” coach Kevin Young said. The defensive effort allowed BYU to take a halftime lead, but the second half became a possession‑by‑possession battle.
